_header.purpose: Web application traffic dashboard showing request rates and active connection metrics
_header.scope: HTTP traffic metrics including request rate by method, active connections, total request counts
_header.overview: Extracts the Traffic row from the golden signals overview into a dedicated detail dashboard. Displays request rate as time series broken down by HTTP method, active connections over time, and stat panels for instant request rate, total request count, and connection count. Enables focused investigation of traffic patterns with drill-down from the Web Summary dashboard.
_header.dependencies: Mimir datasource (uid: mimir), backend http_requests_total counter and http_active_connections gauge
_header.interfaces: PromQL rate and sum queries against http_requests_total and http_active_connections
_header.related: web-summary.json for overview, web-red.json for per-endpoint traffic breakdown
_header.implementation: Grafana dashboard JSON schema version 39 with time series and stat panels for traffic metrics

title: Traffic
description: How much traffic is the backend handling? The left panel shows request rate by HTTP method, the right shows concurrent connections. During an incident, check here first to confirm traffic is actually reaching the backend. A drop to zero means traffic stopped arriving (DNS, ALB, or upstream issue). A spike means a surge or retry storm. Rising connections with flat request rate means requests are queuing — the backend cannot keep up.

panels.title: Request Rate
panels.description: HTTP requests per second broken down by method (GET, POST, etc.). The total line shows overall throughput. Compare against your normal baseline. A sudden cliff means traffic stopped reaching the backend. A spike in POST requests during normal GET-heavy traffic may indicate a retry storm or attack.

panels.title: Active Connections
panels.description: Concurrent in-flight HTTP requests over time. This metric reports the peak concurrent connections per collection interval. Rising connections alongside rising latency means request queuing — the backend is saturated. A flat line at zero between spikes is normal for bursty traffic patterns.

panels.title: Requests / sec
panels.description: Current request throughput. Know your baseline — if this number is significantly lower than normal for this time of day, traffic may not be reaching the backend.

panels.title: Total Requests (range)
panels.description: Cumulative request count for the selected time range. Useful for comparing traffic volume across time windows — widen the range to see if today is lighter or heavier than usual.

panels.title: Active Connections
panels.description: Current peak concurrent connections. This is the saturation signal for traffic. If connections are climbing while request rate is flat, requests are taking longer to complete and piling up.
